sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Calcium channel blockers (CCBs) are a class of medications that lower blood pressure by reducing the amount of calcium entering cells of the heart and blood vessels. They work by blocking calcium from entering through specific channels in these cells, which relaxes blood vessels and reduces the heart's workload. Overdose is associated with vasoplegia and cardiovascular collapse. Overdose can lead to potentially life-threatening cardiovascular, metabolic, and neurological effects.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Hypotension, bradycardia, AV block or other arrhythmias, shock (cardiogenic, distributive)
- Altered mental state, seizures (rare, usually secondary to hypoperfusion)
- Pulmonary oedema, respiratory depression
- Nausea and vomiting, decreased bowel sounds/ileus
- Hyperglycaemia, lactic acidosis, warm, flushed skin

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why or how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm and mixed overdose
- CCB toxicity is potentially life-threatening, particularly with verapamil or diltiazem ingestion
- Patients with underlying cardiovascular disease are at higher risk of severe toxicity
- Co-ingestion of other cardiac medications, such as ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, or digoxin, may worsen toxicity

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if the patient is awake and cooperative
- Position the patient appropriately
- Give oxygen if clinically indicated
- Perform repeated vital signs and BSL
- Consider activated charcoal only if within the appropriate time window and local protocol
- Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor or toxicology support

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management if required
- Consider assisted ventilation if required
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG
- Gain IV access
- Consider i-STAT or other point-of-care testing where available
- Consider fluids only if indicated and reassess frequently
- In severe calcium channel blocker poisoning with hypotension or shock, early toxicology or medical consultation is essential
- Vasopressors and high-dose insulin therapy are key treatments in addition to supportive care
- Consider calcium gluconate in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider atropine, recognising that response may be poor, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider adrenaline in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider high-dose insulin therapy where available and clinically appropriate, in consultation with the on-call doctor or toxicology service
- Consider sodium bicarbonate if metabolic acidosis is evident, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider transthoracic pacing with adequate sedation in consultation with the on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Examples of CCB’s include:**
  - Amlodipine, Nifedipine, Felodipine, Verapamil, Diltiazem
  - Glucagon promotes calcium entry into cells via stimulation of a receptor that is considered to be separate from adrenergic receptors. Glucagon can be considered (in consultation only) in the treatment of CCB for this reason; however, it is unlikely that any site will carry enough Glucagon to follow this through
  - Always offer EAP services where necessary
